coredata.dc:title: Molecular imprinting-based triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor with aggregation-induced emission effect for visual detection of doxycycline
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Jiaqi
Yu, Jialuo
Zhao, Xinming
Chen, Xinru
Zhu, Congzheng
Cao, Xin
Huang, Yan
Li, Bowei
Wu, Yixuan
Chen, Lingxin
Wang, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance sensors for doxycycline (DOX) detection is necessary because its residue accumulation will cause serious harm to human health and the environment. Here, a novel tri-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor was proposed by using “post-mixing” strategy of different emissions fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers with salicylamide as dummy template (DMIPs). BSA was chosen as assistant functional monomer, and also acted as sensitizers for the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect of DOX. The blue-emitting carbon dots and the red-emitting CdTe quantum dots were separately introduced into DMIPs as the response signals. Upon DOX recognition within 2 min, blue and red fluorescence of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor were quenched while green fluorescence of DOX was enhanced, resulting in a wide range of color variations observed over bluish violet-rosered-light pink-orange-yellow-green with a detection limit of 0.061 μM. The sensor possessed highly selective recognition and was successfully applied to detect DOX in complicated real samples. Moreover, with the fluorescent color collection and data processing, the smartphone-assisted visual detection of the sensors showed satisfied sensitivity with low detection limit. This work provides great potential applications for rapid and visual detection of antibiotics in complex substrates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymers
Ratiometric fluorescence
Aggregation-induced emission
Doxycycline
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor
Preparation and characterization of tri-emission DMIPs sensor
Sensitivity and selectivity detection of DOX by tri-emission DMIPs sensor
Practical applicability and method performance comparison

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra and images of BSA and DOX and the mixture of them, respectively. (B) DOX recognition results of DMIPs synthesized in the presence of whether absence of BSA and the image of DOX under 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of b-, g-BSA- and r-DMIPs and the tri-emission DMIPs sensor under 360 nm excitation, respectively, and SEM images of (B) SiO2 nanoparticles and (C, E, G) b-, g-BSA-, and r-DMIPs and (D, F, H) the corresponding b-, g-BSA-, and r-DNIPs.

(A) Response time of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor to DOX. (B) Change of fluorescence intensity ratio of (I485/I410/I626)/(I485/I410/I626)0 with pH. The upper insets of corresponding images display the fluorescence colors under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Fluorescence spectra of tri-emission DMIPs sensor for DOX detection from 0.1 to 50 μM, where the ratio of b-DMIPs: g-BSA-DMIPs: r-DMIPs were (A) 5:5:1, (B) 2:5:1, (C) 6:5:1, (D) 5:6:1, respectively. The upper insets of corresponding images display the fluorescence colors under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Fluorescence spectra and corresponding images of (A) tri-emission DMIPs sensor and (B) DNIPs sensor after adding different concentrations of DOX. The inset graphs show the linear fitting curve of the fluorescence intensity ratio with DOX concentrations of 0.1–50 μM.

The selectivity of tri-emission DMIPs sensor and DNIPs sensor to other antibiotics and interferents and the concentration are all 10 µM. The insets show the corresponding fluorescent images under 365 nm UV lamp (Ser: serine, Thr: threonine, GSH: glutathione, CLDM: clindamycin, SP: sulfomethylpyridine, SMR: sulfamerazine, SA: sulfanilamide, SMZ: sulfamethoxazole, CIP: ciprofloxacin, ENR: enrofloxacin).
Schematic illustration of smartphone APP and linear fitting curve by RGB from APP of tri-emission DMIPs sensor after adding different concentrations of DOX (0.1–50 μM).
Scheme illustration of (A) the preparation and construction strategy for the tri-emission DMIPs sensor and (B) the recognition based on the smartphone APP.
Recoveries and RSDs (%, n = 3) for DOX detection in the spiked real samples by the developed tri-emission DMIPs sensor and HPLC method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average value from three individual experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of DOX in pig excreta samples measured by the tri-emission DMIPs sensor and HPLC method.

head.ce:title: Molecular imprinting-based triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor with aggregation-induced emission effect for visual detection of doxycycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Binzhou Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance sensors for doxycycline (DOX) detection is necessary because its residue accumulation will cause serious harm to human health and the environment. Here, a novel tri-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor was proposed by using “post-mixing” strategy of different emissions fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers with salicylamide as dummy template (DMIPs). BSA was chosen as assistant functional monomer, and also acted as sensitizers for the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect of DOX. The blue-emitting carbon dots and the red-emitting CdTe quantum dots were separately introduced into DMIPs as the response signals. Upon DOX recognition within 2 min, blue and red fluorescence of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor were quenched while green fluorescence of DOX was enhanced, resulting in a wide range of color variations observed over bluish violet-rosered-light pink-orange-yellow-green with a detection limit of 0.061 μM. The sensor possessed highly selective recognition and was successfully applied to detect DOX in complicated real samples. Moreover, with the fluorescent color collection and data processing, the smartphone-assisted visual detection of the sensors showed satisfied sensitivity with low detection limit. This work provides great potential applications for rapid and visual detection of antibiotics in complex substrates.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics belong to broad-spectrum antibiotics, mainly including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), doxycycline (DOX) and so on. They have strong inhibitory effects against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, etc., and are widely applied in clinical medicine and aquaculture because of their spectral antimicrobial properties and affordable cost [49]. Among them, DOX can be used both for the prevention and treatment of diseases, also be added to animal feeds to promote animal growth, due to its significant anti-inflammatory action, longer half-life and high spectral activity [12]. However, the high levels of DOX residues may cause harm to both humans and the environment, such as the risk of intestinal disorders, liver failure, allergic reactions and the spread of drug-resistant strains [32]. For these reasons, the accurate and highly sensitive detection of DOX in complex matrices is particularly important.

Currently, DOX analysis methods mainly include high performance liquid chromatography [16], capillary electrophoresis [17], immunoassay [14] etc. These methods have high selectivity and good separation performance, but they often require long time, expensive laboratory instruments, and professional operation. Therefore, it’s indispensable to develop stable and efficient analytical method with high specificity and rapid identification of DOX. Among them, fluorescence sensing methods are promising for the DOX analysis with the prominent advantages of fast response, low cost, simple operation, high sensitivity, and easy to achieve visual detection [21,45]. Based on the fluorescence enhancement type, the construction of the sensing strategy using molecular characteristics of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) can provide new research ideas for the detection of DOX [28]. The AIE effect can cause molecules that are non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescence to aggregate and emit strong fluorescence [20,25]. DOX has a weak intensity of its own fluorescence emission. DOX molecules contain multiple ketone groups and enol structures in the conjugated system, which can be used as the atypical luminescent molecules to aggregate with external medium environment and adopt appropriate conformations for intermolecular conjugation to take place, thus generating the corresponding fluorescence emission [18,33]. Proteins with a large number of internal hydrophobic cavities have been reported as sensitizers for AIE effect of tetracycline antibiotics, such as bovine serum albumin (BSA) and ovalbumin, etc. [11,26]. DOX can enter the hydrophobic cavities of proteins for aggregation with restricted rotation, thus enhancing the fluorescence emission of DOX by protein sensitization [34]. Based on the self-calibrating mode, ratiometric fluorescence sensors can effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio and achieve reliable quantitative analysis of the target [24,27,42]. The reported ratiometric fluorescence sensors for DOX detection mostly provide color compositions of two emission peaks, however, the color variations of their transitions are still limited even by adjusting the peak ratios. The construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensors by combining multiple fluorescence emission peaks have received increasing attention [6,44], which can widely expand the range of visualized colors change. Therefore, using the AIE effect of DOX to design a rich color change window of multi-fluorescence emission is expected to carry out the innovative work on the visualization and rapid identification of DOX.

Highly selective analysis of DOX is a difficult challenge for ratiometric fluorescence sensors to detect specific trace analytes in complex matrices. In order to reduce/eliminate the matrix effect, the integration of molecular imprinting technology (MIT) on this basis can provide a new idea for the specific recognition of target detection [2,30,8]. Combining fluorescence sensing with MIT, the molecular imprinting-based fluorescence sensors will have both recognition and conduction functions, with excellent sensing performance for highly selective and sensitive detection of targets in complex media [31,4,41]. As mentioned above, BSA can enhance the fluorescence of DOX as a sensitizer, and surprisingly, its effect is not only that. BSA itself is rich in amino and hydroxyl groups, enabling it to be used simultaneously as a functional monomer in the imprinting process [40]. The multifunctional monomer imprinting strategies play an important role in the improving adsorption efficiency and selectivity of molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) for target molecules. Furthermore, the green fluorescence of DOX can be used as the endpoint for visualizing the fluorescence color change, and according to previous literatures [43], the combination of red, green and blue tricolor-emission fluorescence system is the optimal choice for presenting profuse color changes. Based on the RGB color pattern and the different fluorescence response to DOX, two other fluorescent nanomaterials can be screened and combined to construct the triple-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor.

In this work, a novel triple-emission sensing strategy was proposed for DOX naked-eye detection, with the aid of molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor. For the first time, BSA has been acted as a sensitizer to efficiently enhance the fluorescence of DOX, and at the same time played a role of assistant functional monomer to participate in the imprinting process. And the blue-emitting CDs and the red-emitting CdTe QDs were separately introduced into the MIPs as the response signals, respectively. As illustrated in Scheme 1, since the strong interaction between DOX and BAS made the elution of template DOX difficult, we chose salicylamide as the dummy template to prepare the molecularly imprinted polymers (DMIPs) by sol-gel method. Then, according to the “post-mixing” strategy, the fluorescent DMIPs with different emissions were mixed in the suitable ratio to construct the triple-emission molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor, namely, tri-emission DMIPs sensor. Upon the addition of DOX, the blue and red fluorescence were quenched while the green fluorescence of the AIE effect of DOX was enhanced, resulting in a wide range of color variations observed over bluish violet, rosered, light pink, orange, yellow and finally green range, enabling highly sensitive and selective as well as visual detection of DOX. The constructed tri-emission DMIPs sensor was well characterized and its sensing properties and application were also investigated. Furthermore, combining the sensor with a smartphone enabled timely capture and conversion of the fluorescent color information identified by DOX, giving it great potential for application in quantitative DOX detection.
The experimental materials and instruments were provided in the Supporting Information.
SiO2, CdTe QDs and CDs were synthesized according to previous studies [22,37] respectively. The synthesis details were displayed in the Supporting Information.

DMIPs were prepared by a typical sol-gel synthesis method. Briefly, 10 mg of salicylamide was dissolved in 3 mL of ethanol, then added into 15 mL of water and 350 μL of SiO2 aqueous solution (8 mg of SiO2 nanoparticles). Then the mixed solutions were used to prepare DMIPs with different emission respectively, the steps were as follows:

(a) Synthesis of b-DMIPs: followed by 10 μL of APTES, the above mixed solution was stirred for 0.5 h for the pre-polymerization process, then 4 mL of CDs, 20 μL of NH3·H2O and 20 μL of TEOS were added, and the mixture was kept stirred overnight in the dark. (b) Synthesis of g-BSA-DMIPs: followed by 5 μL of APTES, after the mixed solution was stirred for 20 min, 65 mg of BSA and 1 mL of 0.2 M CTAB were added. After stirring for 0.5 h, 50 μL of NH3·H2O and 50 μL of TEOS were added and the mixture was stirred in the dark for 4 h. (c) Synthesis of r-DMIPs: followed by the addition of 5 μL of APTES, and the mixed solution was stirred for 0.5 h, then 5 mL of CdTe QDs solution was added, and the mixture was stirred for another 0.5 h. After that, 50 μL of NH3·H2O and 50 μL of TEOS were transferred into the mixture under stirring overnight in the dark.

Finally, the obtained DMIPs were eluted by ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2, v/v) solution to remove salicylamide and respectively dispersed into 2 mL of aqueous solution for further experiments. As a control, b-DNIPs, g-BSA-DNIPs, r-DNIPs were synthesized using the same procedure but without the addition of salicylamide. Especially, two polymers were also prepared for comparison: the molecularly imprinted and non-imprinted polymers using only APTES as functional monomers (denoted as g-DMIPs and g-DNIPs), using the same procedural approach as g-BSA-DMIPs and g-BSA-DNIPs, but without the addition of BSA during the synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the optimal DOX visualization detection, the tri-emission DMIPs sensor were constructed by mixing the 100 μL of b-DMIPs, 100 μL of g-BSA-DMIPs and 20 μL of r-DMIPs together in 770 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 8.5). Then, 10 μL of DOX solution were added, which the final concentrations of DOX were ranged from 0.1 to 50 μM. After incubation for 2 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were obtained at 400–680 nm with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and a slit of 10/10 nm. The corresponding fluorescence images were subsequently observed under a 365 nm UV lamp and the color information (RGB values) was obtained by the smartphone APP (ColorMeter).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of tri-emission DMIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, b/r-DMIPs and g-BSA-DMIPs were used to construct the multicolor fluorescence sensing system for DOX determination. As presented in Scheme 1, all of the DOX imprinted shell was fabricated on the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles by facile sol–gel polymerization, using APTES as functional monomer and TEOS as cross-linker. The optimization conditions for DMIPs preparation were provided in the Supporting Information. The citric acid synthesized CDs (blue) and the TGA modified CdTe QDs (red) were served as fluorescence sources of b-DMIPs and r-DMIPs, respectively. In addition, since the BSA molecules can give rise to the aggregation of DOX to increasing its fluorescence efficiency ( Fig. 1 A), based on this, we proposed a novel approach to unlock the dual identity of BSA as a sensitizer to enhance the DOX fluorescence while acting as the assistant functional monomer to participate in the g-BSA-DMIPs imprinting process. It can be clearly evident from Fig. 1B that in the same concentration of DOX, the fluorescence intensity of g-BSA-DMIPs was approximately 5 times higher than that for g-DMIPs without BSA, which proved that the design of BSA as an assistant functional monomer was successful and efficient. The mesoporous structure of the imprinted shell ensured that the prepare imprinted cavities involved BSA can be quickly identified for target analytes. In addition, MIPs with DOX as the template molecule was difficult to completely elution due to the strong interaction between BSA and DOX. Therefore, we innovatively selected salicylamide, which is similar to DOX functional group, as the dummy template (Fig. S1) to prepare the DMIPs. After removal of salicylamide, the imprinted cavities were formed. In the presence of DOX, the hydrogen bonding forces were formed between the hydroxyl and amino groups of DOX and the amino groups of APTES in DMIPs, as well as the amino and hydroxyl groups of BSA assistant functional monomer. Based on the special strategies of MIT, a new mode about AIE effect of DOX based on BSA sensitization was proposed, that is, the fluorescence sensing material was prepared by imprinting process, which was simple, stable and effective.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the “post-mixing” strategy was adopted to construct the tri-emission DMIPs sensor by mixing b/r-DMIPs and g-BSA-DMIPs in an appropriate ratio (b-DMIPs: g-BSA-DMIPs: r-DMIPs=5:5:1), which exhibited fluorescence emission peaks at 410 nm and 626 nm under 360 nm excitation. Then, in the presence of DOX, the tri-emission DMIPs sensor with binding sites will sensitively recognize the target. This recognition resulted in the blue/red fluorescence quenching and the green fluorescence enhancement simultaneously, which demonstrate the smartphone-assisted color images changed from bluish violet, rosered, light pink, orange, yellow and finally green (Scheme 1B).

The morphological structures of SiO2, DMIPs and DNIPs were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2B-H, the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles showed a smooth morphology and monodisperse state with the average diameter about 90 nm. Compared to SiO2 nanoparticles, the DMIPs and DNIPs displayed the rougher surface and the thicknesses of imprinted shell layer on the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles were estimated to be approximately 5–8 nm (Fig. S2). The thin imprinted shell layer could provide a large number of specific recognition sites on the surface, which would help to reduce mass transfer resistance and increase the recognition rate and binding capacity. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were further used to better characterize the structure of b-DMIPs and r-DMIPs. As seen in Fig. S3, the XRD pattern of the b-DMIPs showed a broad diffraction peak at 24.3° and this pattern revealed the amorphous surface of b-DMIPs, which was similar to CDs [1]. In addition, the synthesized r-DMIPs exhibited a face-centered cubic structure with peaks assigned to (111), (220) and (311), which confirmed the successful fabrication of r-DMIPs using CdTe QDs [48].

In addition, the main functional groups of SiO2, DMIPs and DNIPs were probed by using FT-IR spectroscopy. As observed in the Fig. S4, the characteristic peaks at 790 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Si-O, the vibration peak at 1105 cm−1 was corresponding to the Si-O-Si stretching vibration, all of which indicated that the silicon sphere was successfully introduced in these materials. The broad absorption band at 3455 cm−1 could be rooted in the O-H stretching vibration of SiO2, DMIPs, DNIPs as well as the N-H stretching vibrations of APTES. The COO− symmetric stretching vibration peak was observed at 1420 cm−1 [29], confirming the introduction of carboxyl groups in the citric acid synthesized CDs, TGA modified CdTe QDs and BSA, respectively. And the appearance of new bands at 1550 cm−1 from the amide II (C-N stretching and NH bending) was further indicated the presence of imprinting layers [9]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the prepared DMIPs were also recorded to investigate the surface of component and gain more structure information, respectively. It can be observed from Fig. S5, as for b-DMIPs, the C 1 s core-level XPS spectrum can be decomposed into three peaks at 284.7 eV (C–C/CC), 286.6 eV (C–O) and 288.5 eV (CO) [15]. The XPS spectra of N 1 s (Fig. S5C) at 398.7 eV, 399.2 eV and 401.1 eV were assigned to N–H, N = C, and N − C bonds [36], respectively, and coincides as well with FT-IR results. The Fig. S5D showed that signals of Si 2p at 106.3 eV, S 2p at 175 eV, C1s at 286.7 eV, N1s at 400 eV and O1s 531.8 eV, which illustrated the g-BSA-DMIPs were composed of Si, S, C, N and O. The XPS spectra of r-DMIPs were also recorded to analyze the surface chemical compositions and gain more structure information. In the spectrum (Fig. S5G), the elements of C, N, O, and Si could be clearly seen and the signals of Cd and Te were very weak, which indicated CdTe QDs were embedded in the inside of r-DMIPs layer [19].

As shown in Fig. S6, the signal of the energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) analysis was further used to determine the composition of the b-DMIPs and r-DMIPs. According to this assay, the synthesized b-DMIPs possessed C, O and Si atoms while the synthesized r-DMIPs possessed C, O, Cd, Te, S and Si atoms, revealing that the CDs and CdTe QDs were used as the fluorescent signal sources to participate in the imprinting process and the ultrathin imprinted shell layers were prepared on the surface of SiO2, as we were expected. Furthermore, elemental analysis was carried out to examine N, C and S content for verify the existence of BSA (Table S1), which were consistent with the results of XPS. As shown, the higher percentage of S (2.22%), and C (40.01%) elements in g-BSA-DMIPs compared to g-BSA-DNIPs. This due to the elution of template molecules, g-BSA-DMIPs has a larger number of imprinting cavities than g-BSA-DNIPs, so more BSA can be grafted into g-BSA-DMIPs. Also, as expected, g-DMIPs didn’t have the S element because BSA was not added during the imprinting process. These suggesting that BSA was successfully introduced to g-BSA-DMIPs.

The different emission peaks were examined separately to explore the possible mechanisms involved the tri-emission DMIPs sensor. As shown in Fig. S7, the excitation wavelength of the b-DMIPs sensor overlaped with the UV absorption peak of DOX in the range of 270–360 nm. Therefore, it was hypothesized that the mechanism causing the fluorescence emission peak quenching at 410 nm could be the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [50]. The average fluorescence lifetime of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor at 410 nm was 0.97 ns with the addition of DOX and 1.29 ns without DOX (Fig. S8A). The approximately constant fluorescence lifetime was suggested that the IFE sensing process may be responsible for the quenching of the blue fluorescence in the DMIPs sensor.

Subsequently, we discussed the reason for the enhanced fluorescence emission peak at 485 nm which was the result of the AIE effect on DOX. It was worth noting that BSA protein molecule has the three-dimensional hydrophobic cavities [7], which can act as a sensitizer and bind to DOX through intermolecular interactions [47]. DOX has abundant electrostatic regions and donor or acceptor groups for hydrogen bonding, so it’s an excellent choice for interaction with BSA molecules. When DOX molecules entered the hydrophobic cavity of BSA, it leaded to the aggregation of DOX molecules and restricted their rotational motion, thus the fluorescence was significantly enhanced (Fig. 1 A). Thus, the sensitizer BSA was able to bind specifically to DOX as the assistant functional monomer in the imprinted sites of DMIPs, achieving a significant enhancement of DOX fluorescence intensity and contributing to the efficient recognition of DOX.

Then, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the r-DMIPs was also investigated. As revealed by the UV absorption spectrum of DOX and fluorescence spectrum of r-DMIPs in Fig. S9, there was no spectral overlap between them, which indicated that FRET wouldn’t be the main pathway of the fluorescence quenching mechanism [38]. As depicted in Fig. S8B, the average fluorescence lifetime of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor at 626 nm of the red fluorescence peak (19 ns) was significantly longer than that after the addition of DOX (16 ns), which implied that the quenching mechanism of the red fluorescence peak was dynamic quenching, and the IFE and static quenching can be excluded. In addition, the dynamic quenching can be verified by whether the slope of the Stern–Volmer equation varies with different temperature. As shown in Fig. S10, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of QDs before and after adding DOX, respectively. The result showed that the slope of the Stern–Volmer plot increased as the temperature increased, further testifying the quenching process followed the dynamic quenching [35]. Hence, the detection mechanism of DOX by the tri-emission DMIPs sensor may be the results of the combining of the IFE, protein sensitization, and dynamic quenching.

The experimental parameters of pH and response time of tri-emission DMIPs sensor were examined and optimized prior to the sensitivity and selectivity analysis. When the response time examination was performed, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I485/I410/I626)/(I485/I410/I626)0 was obtained within 2 min of 10 μM DOX addition and reached stability quickly, as shown in Fig. 3 A. Owning to the thin-imprinted shell prepared by the MIT, the efficiency of DOX recognition to fluorescence signal output was greatly improved, so that the response equilibrium can be reached immediately and remained stable during the following 60 min. Therefore, 2 min was set as the optimized response time for the tri-emission DMIPs sensor to detect DOX. In addition, the effect of pH on the detection performance of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor after the addition of DOX was considered. The optimal sensitization of BAS for DOX occurred under alkaline conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I485/I410/I626)/(I485/I410/I626)0 was maximum at pH 8.5 and the color change was most pronounced when observed by the naked eye, so pH 8.5 was chosen as the optimal pH condition for subsequent experiments.

Since the tri-emission DMIPs sensor was constructed using the “post-mixing” strategy, it was crucial to optimize the ratio between b-DMIPs, g-BSA-DMIPs, and r-DMIPs to achieve the desired visualization effect. As shown in the Fig. S11, after the addition of DOX, the fluorescence color of b-DMIPs was almost always blue in the concentration range of 0.1–50 μM, with a slight darkening of the brightness at the end point; g-BSA-DMIPs showed a clear change from colorless to bright green with the increase of DOX concentration; it was worth mentioning that r-DMIPs, under the influence of green fluorescence of DOX itself, the color of the solution gradually changed from red to orange as the concentration of DOX increases. The critical control point in the construction of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor was to change the volume ratio between b-, g-BSA-, r-DMIPs to identify the maximum color abundance. To achieve the best DOX visual detection, 100 μL of b-DMIPs, 100 μL of g-BSA- DMIPs and 20 of μL r-DMIPs were selected to constructed the tri-emission DMIPs sensor, as the volume ratio of 5:5:1, which showed satisfied wide color changed from bluish violet-rosered-light pink-orange-yellow-green under 365 nm UV lamp ( Fig. 4A). The green emission peak of sensor gradually enhanced, with green as the endpoint color, as well as red and blue peaks were quenched, which is the main cause of the color change. If too little b-DMIPs was added, like the volume ratio of b-DMIPs: g-BSA-DMIPs: r-DMIPs = 2:5:1, the initial color of the solution was more reddish, and the presence of blue fluorescence can hardly be observed (Fig. 4B). If more b-DMIPs was added (volume ratio 6:5:1), the content of r-DMIPs was relatively reduced, the color transition of the solution was not obvious before the appearance of green (Fig. 4C). When less g-BSA-DMIPs was added, there was no doubt that the solution endpoint cannot be green and the original purpose of the triple-emission fluorescence sensor was lost. Conversely, when more g-BSA-DMIPs were added, the solution rapidly changed to green, exhibiting a bluish-violet-wheat-green color change with fewer transition colors and repeated endpoint color (b-DMIPs: g-BSA-DMIPs: r-DMIPs =5:6:1), as shown in Fig. 4D. Therefore, the volume ratio of b-DMIPs: g-BSA-DMIPs: r-DMIPs = 5:5:1 was determined as the optimal ratio for the constructed tri-emission DMIPs sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity detection of DOX by tri-emission DMIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the analytical detection performance of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor, the DOX linear range and the limit of detection (LOD) were investigated. The fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of DOX interacting with the tri-emission DMIPs sensor under the above optimal experimental conditions were shown in Fig. 5. Before the addition of DOX, the tri-emission DMIPs sensor only emitted two fluorescence emission peaks at 410 and 626 nm under 360 nm excitation. With the DOX increasing, the fluorescence emission peaks at 410 and 626 nm were quenched, while the green fluorescence emission of DOX sensitized by BSA was continuously enhanced. The fluorescence intensities of the triple emission peaks were varied due to their simultaneous increase and decrease, resulting in a wide range of color variations with rich transitions. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the tri-emission DMIPs sensor was clearly observed to change from the initial bluish violet, rosered, light pink, orange, yellow and finally green (inset of the Fig. 5A), and the Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram (Fig. S12) also illustrated the fluorescence color shift, which could be easily recognized by the naked eye.

Based on the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (I485/I410/I626)/(I485/I410/I626)0 displayed a good linear relationship to DOX concentrations in the range of 0.1–50 μM, with corresponding correlation coefficient R
2 = 0.995 (Fig. 5A). The LOD of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor toward DOX was calculated to 0.061 μM on the basis of 3σ/s (σ: standard deviation of the blank sample, s: slope of the standard curve). In contrast, as seen in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence color of DNIPs sensor didn’t change significantly, especially the green fluorescence peak at 485 nm changed weakly. The different spectra and color changes of DMIPs and DNIPs sensors further proved that the existence and efficiency of the imprinting cavities. The presence of template in the process of imprinting polymerization allowed for successful and efficient preparation of recognition sites, even when using a dummy template, which can specifically capture DOX and cause the sensitively response of fluorescence signal. The lack of selective recognition sites in DNIPs layers resulted in the little interaction with DOX, and thus color of the fluorescence changed only slightly. In addition, for well evaluating the imprinting effect, an imprinting factor of 2.7 was obtained, which was defined as the ratio of the slope of the two standard curves for DMIPs and DNIPs sensors. Hence, it could be reasonably inferred that the tri-emission DMIPs sensor based wide visualization range had the capability in rapid DOX analysis with high sensitivity and precision.

In selectivity study, the analytical performance of tri-emission DMIPs sensor was examined in the presence of various relevant substances including representative antibiotics (TCs antibiotics, quinolone antibiotics, sulfonamide antibiotics), amino acids (serine, threonine), glutathione and some heavy metal ions (Cd2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+). The experimental results were presented in Fig. 6, as we can see, the (I485/I410/I626)/(I485/I410/I626)0 value of DOX detection was much higher than those of other substances, indicating excellent selectivity to DOX. From the color discussion, the fluorescence color of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor had an obviously response toward DOX with the color varied from bluish violet to orange. Moreover, other antibiotics in TCs showed slight color changes, while the fluorescence color change of other substrates was hard to catch by the naked eye. Because TC, OTC and CTC belong to the same class of TCs antibiotics as DOX and have similar structures and effects, that was why they also showed a certain fluorescence response to the tri-emission DMIPs sensor. It was worth noting that DNIPs shown very low affinity toward DOX and other analogs with no changing homogeneous bluish violet color, which proved the obvious effect of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor compared with DNIPs sensor. The DMIPs layers contained numerous recognition sites that complement the shape or function of target molecules, resulting in high selectivity even in complex substrates. These results demonstrated that the prepared tri-emission DMIPs sensor can specifically recognize tetracyclines antibiotics with excellent selectivity for DOX detection.

The superiority of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor in sensitivity and selectivity was further confirmed by comparing it with the single-emission sensors. As listed in Table S2, the comparison of LOD over different DMIPs revealed that the sensitivity of g-BSA-DMIPs had the best sensitivity of 0.088 μM, but it was not as good as the tri-emission DMIPs sensor, and the single fluorescence color change visualization was not ideal. In addition, as shown in Fig. S13, we can find that in the selectivity of b-DMIPs, the fluorescence responses were stronger for DOX, CTC and OTC, relatively poorer for TC, and lower for other interferents. The fluorescence enhancing amount of the g-BSA-DMIPs was the highest for DOX, which was twice that of CTC, 4 times that of TC and OTC, and much higher than other interferents. In the selectivity experiment of r-DMIPs, the response to DOX, OTC and other substances showed a certain fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, based on the individual fluorescence performance of each DMIPs, we suspected that g-BSA-DMIPs will play a major role in the fluorescent sensing of the tri-emission DMIP sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applicability and method performance comparison
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of practical DOX detection in complex matrices were investigated using environmental and biological real samples. The recoveries for seawater, reservoir water and urine were assessed by the determination of DOX at three different spiked concentrations (3, 10 and 30 μM), respectively. As listed in Table 1, the DOX recoveries were attained in the range of 91.6–107.5% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.9–4.6% for the spiked samples. In addition, as can be seen from Table 2, it was clear that the DMIPs sensor was capable of detecting DOX in real samples from pig excreta at the concentrations of 0.748–2.051 μM. Moreover, the accuracy of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor was verified using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, and the values of E% [10] were given as shown in Table S3-4. It can be seen that the experimental results taken by DMIPs sensor were in good agreement with the conventional HPLC detection results, which indicated the prepared method can be well applied to real samples determination with high accuracy.

In addition, the strategy and performance of our developed DMIPs sensor for detecting DOX was compared with other previous reported fluorescence detection methods, as displayed in Table S5 [13,23,3,39,46,5]. It was noteworthy that the tri-emission DMIPs sensor in this study has a lower LOD and the wide detection range. More excitingly, the ratiometric fluorescence system based on molecular imprinting provided profuse color evolution for the DOX sensing in short response time. Through the simple proportional regulation of the “post-mixing” process, the constructed tri-emission DMIPs sensor provided the new method for realizing convenient and fast visual fluorescence TCs antibiotics detection.

To achieve rapid and convenient detection of DOX, the tri-emission DMIPs sensor underwent a successive color change that can be directly observed and further achieved data processing by smartphone. The color changes can be directly converted into RGB values by using the digital processing power of the smartphone APP (ColorMeter). After capturing the fluorescence images, the fluorescence color of the tri-emission DMIPs sensor was changed from bluish violet to green with the addition of the different concentrations of DOX and then the RGB values were obtained. Fig. 7 showed that in the concentration range of 0.1–50 μM, the ratio of (G/B/R)/(G/B/R)0 and the concentration of DOX had a good linear relationship with the corresponding linear equations of y = 0.0582x+ 1.0391, R
2 = 0.992, respectively. Meanwhile, the LOD was calculated as 0.071 μM, which was similar to the fluorescence analysis. In consequence, the above satisfying results indicated that the tri-emission DMIPs sensor possessed the high sensitivity and visual analysis capability combined with smartphone APP.

In summary, a tri-emission molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor was successfully constructed by using a “post-mixing” strategy of the b-, g-BSA- and r-DMIPs in an optimized ratio to achieve the visual detection of DOX. Noteworthily, combined with the multifunctional monomer imprinting strategies to improve selectivity, BSA as the assistant functional monomer can also serve as a sensitizer, with strong sensitization causing AIE effects of DOX. This tri-emission DMIPs sensor recognized DOX with quenching of the blue and red fluorescence at 410 and 626 nm along with enhancing the green fluorescence emission of DOX, accompanied by obvious fluorescence color changes from bluish violet, rosered, light pink, orange, yellow and finally green. The sensor based wide visualization range had the high sensitivity and selectivity in rapid DOX analysis with excellent practicability. Furthermore, the corresponding color changes of the sensor can be converted into RGB values through the smartphone APP to achieve the visual quantitative analysis of DOX. This work is expected to promote further research on improving affinity and specificity of molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors. The development of new sensing methods with wide color variations, high efficiency and strong anti-interference advantages will help to expand the application of visual fluorescence sensors for real-time and on-site detection, so as to provide a universal analysis platform for the identification of emerging pollutants such as antibiotics in the future.

Doxycycline (DOX) has been used for the prevention and treatment of diseases, and can also be added to animal feeds to promote animal growth. However, owing to its incomplete degradation, the continued accumulation of DOX residues will have the serious impacts on human health and the environment. To detect trace DOX in complex matrices, a molecular imprinting-based triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed by using a “post-mixing” strategy, enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of DOX with a wide range of color variations observed. The developed sensor provides a promising approach in DOX determination and environmental protection.

Xinming Zhao: Investigation. Jialuo Yu: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Xin Cao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinru Chen: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Yan Huang: Validation, Formal analysis. Yixuan Wu: Validation, Visualization. Bowei Li: Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Congzheng Zhu: Investigation, Validation. Xiaoyan Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lingxin Chen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Jiaqi Miao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804010, 41776110, 22376216, 42376188), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023MB021, ZR2020QB105), Taishan Scholar Project Special Funding (Grant No. ts20190962).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.134218.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
